<script>
(function(){
  function renderAll(){
    if(typeof renderMathInElement!=='function'){ return setTimeout(renderAll,50); }
    renderMathInElement(document.body,{
      delimiters:[
        {left:'$$',right:'$$',display:true},
        {left:'$',right:'$',display:false},
        {left:'\\(',right:'\\)',display:false},
        {left:'\\[',right:'\\]',display:true}
      ],
      throwOnError:false
    });
  }
  if(document.readyState==='loading') document.addEventListener('DOMContentLoaded',renderAll);
  else renderAll();
})();
</script>
<style>
  :root{
    --bg:#0f1218; --panel:#161b24; --panel2:#1c2230;
    --ink:#e8ecf1; --mute:#9aa4b2; --line:#2a3242;
    --yellow:#ffd866; --blue:#58c4dd; --green:#83c167;
    --pink:#e07a5f; --violet:#b39ddb; --cyan:#7de0d6;
  }
  *{box-sizing:border-box}
  html,body{margin:0;padding:0;background:var(--bg);color:var(--ink);
    font-family:-apple-system,BlinkMacSystemFont,"Segoe UI",Inter,Roboto,sans-serif;
    font-size:17px;line-height:1.65}
  a{color:var(--blue)}
  code{font-family:ui-monospace,SFMono-Regular,Menlo,Consolas,monospace;
    background:#0b0f16;padding:1px 6px;border-radius:4px;color:#f0c987;font-size:0.92em}
  main{max-width:880px;margin:0 auto;padding:4rem 1.5rem 8rem}
  h1{font-size:2.4rem;letter-spacing:-0.01em;margin:0 0 0.4rem}
  h2{font-size:1.6rem;margin:3.2rem 0 0.8rem;color:#fff;border-bottom:1px solid var(--line);padding-bottom:.4rem}
  h3{font-size:1.15rem;margin:1.8rem 0 .6rem;color:var(--yellow)}
  p{margin:0.7rem 0}
  .sub{color:var(--mute);margin-top:0;margin-bottom:2rem}
  .widget{background:var(--panel);border:1px solid var(--line);border-radius:10px;
    padding:1rem 1.1rem 1.2rem;margin:1.2rem 0}
  .widget .hd{display:flex;justify-content:space-between;align-items:baseline;margin-bottom:.6rem;gap:.8rem;flex-wrap:wrap}
  .widget .hd .ttl{font-weight:600;color:#fff;font-size:.95rem;letter-spacing:.02em;text-transform:uppercase}
  .widget .hd .hint{color:var(--mute);font-size:.85rem}
  .row{display:flex;gap:.6rem;flex-wrap:wrap;align-items:center;margin:.4rem 0}
  .row label{color:var(--mute);font-size:.88rem}
  input[type="text"],input[type="number"],select{
    background:#0b0f16;color:var(--ink);border:1px solid var(--line);
    border-radius:6px;padding:.35rem .55rem;font-family:inherit;font-size:.95rem}
  input[type="range"]{accent-color:var(--yellow);flex:1;max-width:260px}
  button{background:var(--panel2);color:var(--ink);border:1px solid var(--line);
    border-radius:6px;padding:.35rem .7rem;cursor:pointer;font:inherit}
  button:hover{background:#242c3e}
  button.active{background:var(--yellow);color:#111;border-color:var(--yellow)}
  .readout{background:#0b0f16;border:1px solid var(--line);border-radius:6px;
    padding:.6rem .8rem;font-family:ui-monospace,monospace;font-size:.92rem;color:#cbd5e1;
    min-height:1.6em;white-space:pre-wrap;word-break:break-word}
  .pill{display:inline-block;background:var(--panel2);border:1px solid var(--line);
    border-radius:999px;padding:1px 8px;font-size:.82rem;color:var(--mute);margin-right:4px}
  .note{border-left:3px solid var(--violet);padding:.3rem .9rem;color:#d8d2ef;background:#1a1828;
    margin:1rem 0;border-radius:0 6px 6px 0}
  .ok{border-left:3px solid var(--green);padding:.3rem .9rem;color:#d5ebc8;background:#1a2418;
    margin:1rem 0;border-radius:0 6px 6px 0}
  .bad{border-left:3px solid var(--pink);padding:.3rem .9rem;color:#f4d2c7;background:#2a1a18;
    margin:1rem 0;border-radius:0 6px 6px 0}
  svg{display:block;margin:0 auto;max-width:100%}
  svg text{fill:var(--ink);font-family:inherit}
  nav.toc{position:sticky;top:0;background:rgba(15,18,24,.93);backdrop-filter:blur(8px);
    border-bottom:1px solid var(--line);padding:.6rem 1rem;z-index:10;
    display:flex;gap:1rem;flex-wrap:wrap;font-size:.9rem}
  nav.toc a{color:var(--mute);text-decoration:none}
  nav.toc a:hover{color:var(--yellow)}
  /* sticky sidebar TOC (wide screens only; narrow screens fall back to top-nav) */
  aside.sidetoc{position:fixed;top:4.2rem;
    left:max(1rem, calc(50% - 440px - 220px));
    width:200px;max-height:calc(100vh - 6rem);overflow-y:auto;
    padding-right:1rem;border-right:1px solid var(--line);
    font-size:.88rem;z-index:5}
  aside.sidetoc .sidetoc-title{font-size:.7rem;letter-spacing:.14em;text-transform:uppercase;
    color:var(--mute);margin:0 0 .5rem;padding-bottom:.35rem;border-bottom:1px solid var(--line)}
  aside.sidetoc ol{list-style:none;padding:0;margin:0}
  aside.sidetoc li{margin:0}
  aside.sidetoc a{display:block;padding:.3rem .5rem;color:var(--mute);text-decoration:none;
    border-left:2px solid transparent;line-height:1.35;
    transition:color 120ms ease, border-color 120ms ease}
  aside.sidetoc a:hover{color:var(--ink)}
  aside.sidetoc a.active{color:var(--yellow);border-left-color:var(--yellow)}
  @media (max-width:1180px){ aside.sidetoc{display:none} }
  @media (min-width:1181px){ nav.toc a[href^="#"]{display:none} }
  .hero{padding:3rem 0 1rem}
  .small{font-size:.88rem;color:var(--mute)}
  footer{color:var(--mute);font-size:.88rem;text-align:center;margin-top:4rem}
  table.plain{width:100%;border-collapse:collapse;font-size:.95rem;margin:.6rem 0}
  table.plain th,table.plain td{padding:.35rem .5rem;border-bottom:1px solid var(--line);text-align:left}
  table.plain th{color:var(--mute);font-weight:500}
  aside.callback{
    margin:1.2rem 0;padding:.7rem 1rem;
    background:rgba(88,196,221,0.05);
    border-left:3px solid color-mix(in srgb, var(--cyan) 45%, transparent);
    border-radius:0 6px 6px 0;
    font-size:.93rem;
  }
  aside.callback .ttl{
    font-size:.68rem;letter-spacing:.14em;text-transform:uppercase;
    color:var(--mute,#8c9aa6);margin-bottom:.3rem;font-weight:600;
  }
  aside.callback ul{margin:0;padding-left:1.1rem}
  aside.callback ul li{margin:.15rem 0}
  aside.callback a{color:inherit}
  details.changelog{
    margin:2rem 0 1rem;padding:.6rem .9rem;
    background:rgba(0,0,0,0.22);
    border-radius:6px;
    font-size:.85rem;
  }
  details.changelog summary{
    cursor:pointer;letter-spacing:.1em;text-transform:uppercase;
    font-size:.7rem;color:var(--mute,#8c9aa6);
  }
  details.changelog table{margin-top:.5rem;border-collapse:collapse;width:100%;font-size:.82rem}
  details.changelog td{padding:.2rem .6rem;border-bottom:1px dashed rgba(255,255,255,0.07);vertical-align:top}
  details.changelog td:first-child{color:var(--mute,#8c9aa6);white-space:nowrap;font-variant-numeric:tabular-nums}
  aside.related{
    margin:1.2rem 0;padding:.7rem 1rem;
    background:color-mix(in srgb, var(--mute) 6%, transparent);
    border-left:3px solid color-mix(in srgb, var(--mute) 55%, transparent);
    border-radius:0 6px 6px 0;
    font-size:.93rem;
  }
  aside.related .ttl{
    font-size:.68rem;letter-spacing:.14em;text-transform:uppercase;
    color:var(--mute,#8c9aa6);margin-bottom:.3rem;font-weight:600;
  }
  aside.related > div{margin:.15rem 0}
  aside.related .more{color:var(--mute);font-style:italic}
  aside.related a{color:inherit}
  /* display-prefs-css-auto-begin */
  html[data-hide-widgets] .widget { display: none !important; }
  html[data-hide-quizzes] .quiz { display: none !important; }
  .mv-display-toggles { display: inline-flex; gap: .25rem; }
  .mv-display-toggle { background: transparent; border: 1px solid var(--line, #333); border-radius: 4px; padding: 2px 6px; cursor: pointer; font-size: 1rem; line-height: 1; color: inherit; }
  .mv-display-toggle:hover { background: var(--panel, rgba(255,255,255,0.05)); }
  .mv-display-toggle--off { opacity: 0.4; }
  /* display-prefs-css-auto-end */
</style>
<!-- display-prefs-head-auto-begin -->
<script src="./js/display-prefs.js"></script>
<!-- display-prefs-head-auto-end -->
</head>
rawBodyPrefix:
вҶҗ Notebook
1 Why forms? 2 1-forms 3 Wedge & k-forms 4 Exterior derivative 5 Stokes 6 de Rham 7 Connections

Differential forms вҖ” $d$, Stokes, and de Rham

Forms are the things you integrate. They eat tangent vectors, return numbers, and transform correctly under pullback so that integration over a manifold doesn't depend on coordinates. This page follows the arc from 1-forms through the exterior derivative, Stokes's theorem, and the first whisper of cohomology вҖ” the failure of closed forms to be exact.

blocks.html:
1. Why forms?

Calculus on $\mathbb{R}^n$ has three kinds of "fields" that students meet early: scalar functions $f$, vector fields $X$, and вҖ” if one is lucky вҖ” the gradient $\nabla f$, the curl $\nabla \times X$, and the divergence $\nabla \cdot X$. These are useful but coordinate-bound: the notation hides that $\nabla f$ is a different kind of object from $X$, and the curl only makes sense in three dimensions.

A differential $k$-form on a smooth manifold $M$ is a smooth section of $\Lambda^k T^*M$. Unpacked: at each point $p\in M$ it is an alternating multilinear map

$\omega_p \colon T_pM \times \cdots \times T_pM \to \mathbb{R}$ ($k$ factors).

It eats $k$ tangent vectors and gives a number, and the number flips sign when any two inputs are swapped.

Forms are the things you integrate. A $k$-form on $M^n$ is exactly the algebraic object that can be integrated over a $k$-dimensional oriented submanifold. This is not a coincidence; it is the whole reason for the antisymmetry. Changing coordinates produces a Jacobian determinant, which is precisely the alternating multilinear thing you need.
objecteatsintegrated over
$0$-form (function) $f$nothing$0$-dim submanifolds (points, with signs)
$1$-form $\omega$one tangent vectorcurves
$2$-form $\omega$two tangent vectorssurfaces
$k$-form $\omega$$k$ tangent vectors$k$-dim oriented submanifolds
top form ($k=\dim M$)$n$ tangent vectors$M$ itself

The grand payoff is Stokes's theorem, $\int_{\partial M}\omega = \int_M d\omega$, which makes the fundamental theorem of calculus, Green, Stokes (classical), and Gauss-divergence into a single statement. And the kernel-versus-image game for the operator $d$ вҖ” closed modulo exact вҖ” is a topological invariant of $M$: de Rham cohomology.

A quick contrast

  • Scalar fields: functions $M\to\mathbb{R}$. These are $0$-forms.
  • Vector fields: sections of $TM$. Dual to $1$-forms via a metric, but not the same thing.
  • $1$-forms (covectors): sections of $T^*M$. These are what you integrate along curves. In coordinates, $\omega = \sum_i \omega_i(x)\,dx^i$.

On $\mathbb{R}^n$ with its Euclidean metric the distinction between vector fields and $1$-forms is papered over: physicists write $\vec{F}\cdot d\vec{r}$, mathematicians write $\omega$. Once a metric is in the picture, vectors and covectors can be raised and lowered. But the algebra вҖ” wedge, $d$, Stokes вҖ” lives purely on the covector side, and that is where coordinate-freeness comes from.

2. 1-forms and the differential $df$

Fix $p\in M$. A covector at $p$ is a linear functional $T_pM\to\mathbb{R}$. The dual space $T_p^*M$ has the same dimension as $T_pM$. A $1$-form is a smooth choice of covector at every point. In coordinates $(x^1,\ldots,x^n)$ the basis $1$-forms $dx^1,\ldots,dx^n$ are defined by

$dx^i\!\left(\partial/\partial x^j\right) = \delta^i_j,$

and any $1$-form can be written uniquely as $\omega = \omega_i(x)\,dx^i$ (summation convention). The action on a tangent vector $v = v^j\,\partial/\partial x^j$ is just $\omega(v) = \omega_i v^i$.

The differential of a function

For a smooth function $f\colon M\to\mathbb{R}$, the differential $df$ is the $1$-form whose value on a vector $v$ is the directional derivative:

$df_p(v) = v(f) = \frac{d}{dt}\Big|_{t=0} f(\gamma(t))$

for any curve $\gamma$ with $\gamma(0)=p$, $\dot\gamma(0)=v$. In coordinates, $df = \sum_i \frac{\partial f}{\partial x^i}\,dx^i$ вҖ” exactly the "total differential" from multivariable calculus, but now honestly typed as a covector.

Picture. A $1$-form $\omega$ at a point can be pictured as a family of equally spaced level hyperplanes in $T_pM$. The value $\omega(v)$ counts (with sign) how many of those hyperplanes the vector $v$ pierces. The kernel of $\omega$ вҖ” vectors tangent to the level hyperplanes вҖ” is where $\omega$ vanishes. For $df$, the level hyperplanes are the tangent planes to level sets of $f$, and $df(v)$ measures how fast $f$ grows in the direction $v$.
Widget В· 1-form evaluatordrag the blue vector
1 0
The dashed yellow lines are a few "level lines" of the covector $\omega_p$ in the tangent plane вҖ” evenly spaced sets of constant $\omega(\cdot)$. The cyan line through the basepoint is $\ker\omega_p$. The blue arrow is your tangent vector $v$; the readout is $\omega_p(v)$.

3. The wedge product and $k$-forms

Given two $1$-forms $\alpha,\beta$ on $M$, their wedge product $\alpha\wedge\beta$ is the $2$-form defined by

$(\alpha\wedge\beta)(v,w) = \alpha(v)\beta(w) - \alpha(w)\beta(v).$

It is bilinear and antisymmetric: $\alpha\wedge\beta = -\beta\wedge\alpha$, and in particular $\alpha\wedge\alpha = 0$. Antisymmetry is exactly what makes $(\alpha\wedge\beta)(v,w)$ a signed area: the action on the parallelogram spanned by $v,w$ is a determinant in the $(\alpha,\beta)$ components.

More generally, $\Omega^k(M) := \Gamma(\Lambda^k T^*M)$ is the space of smooth $k$-forms, and

$\wedge\colon \Omega^k\otimes\Omega^\ell \to \Omega^{k+\ell}$

is bilinear, associative, and graded-commutative: $\alpha\wedge\beta = (-1)^{k\ell}\,\beta\wedge\alpha$. A basis of $\Omega^k(\mathbb{R}^n)$ over $C^\infty(\mathbb{R}^n)$ is

$\{\,dx^{i_1}\wedge\cdots\wedge dx^{i_k}\ :\ 1\le i_1

so $\dim \Omega^k(\mathbb{R}^n) = \binom{n}{k}$. In particular $\Omega^k=0$ for $k>n$, and $\Omega^n$ is one-dimensional (spanned by the volume form $dx^1\wedge\cdots\wedge dx^n$).

Antisymmetry = oriented-area. Plugging the same vector in twice into any alternating form gives $0$. Swapping inputs flips sign. So a $2$-form is the data of a signed area on every $2$-plane of tangent vectors вҖ” a "ruler for parallelograms." A $k$-form is a ruler for oriented $k$-parallelepipeds.
Widget В· 2-form on a parallelogramdrag v (blue) and w (pink)
The parallelogram is filled with a color whose sign reflects $\omega(v,w)$: green if positive, pink if negative. The magnitude $|\omega(v,w)|$ is the scalar-multiple of the unsigned parallelogram area. Swapping $v\leftrightarrow w$ flips the sign.
See also
  • Tangent spaces
Used in
Differential forms В· Exterior derivative
Differential forms В· Pullback of forms
Dynamical systems В· Conservative vs dissipative flows
Riemannian geometry В· Riemannian volume form and divergence

</section>

<script>
(function(){
  const svg = document.getElementById('w2-svg');
  const out = document.getElementById('w2-out');
  const pick = document.getElementById('w2-pick');
  const swap = document.getElementById('w2-swap');
  if(!svg) return;
  const W=520,H=340;
  const cx=W/2, cy=H/2, sc=60;
  const P = {x:0, y:0};
  let V = {x:1.6, y:0.4};
  let Wv = {x:0.5, y:1.4};

  function toScr(mx,my){ return [cx + mx*sc, cy - my*sc]; }
  function fromScr(sx,sy){ return [(sx-cx)/sc, -(sy-cy)/sc]; }

  let drag=null;
  svg.addEventListener('mousedown', e=>{
    const r = svg.getBoundingClientRect();
    const sx = (e.clientX-r.left)*(W/r.width);
    const sy = (e.clientY-r.top)*(H/r.height);
    const pv = toScr(P.x+V.x, P.y+V.y), pw = toScr(P.x+Wv.x, P.y+Wv.y);
    if(Math.hypot(pv[0]-sx, pv[1]-sy) < 24) drag='v';
    else if(Math.hypot(pw[0]-sx, pw[1]-sy) < 24) drag='w';
    if(drag) e.preventDefault();
  });
  window.addEventListener('mouseup', ()=>{ drag=null; });
  window.addEventListener('mousemove', e=>{
    if(!drag) return;
    const r = svg.getBoundingClientRect();
    const sx = (e.clientX-r.left)*(W/r.width);
    const sy = (e.clientY-r.top)*(H/r.height);
    const [mx,my] = fromScr(sx,sy);
    if(drag==='v'){ V.x = mx - P.x; V.y = my - P.y; }
    else { Wv.x = mx - P.x; Wv.y = my - P.y; }
    render();
  });

  swap.addEventListener('click', ()=>{
    const t = V; V = Wv; Wv = t; render();
  });

  function coef(){
    const k = pick.value;
    if(k==='dxdy') return 1;
    if(k==='2dxdy') return 2;
    if(k==='xdxdy') return P.x; // value at basepoint
    if(k==='neg') return -1;
    return 1;
  }
  function label(){
    return {dxdy:'dx вҲ§ dy', '2dxdy':'2В·dx вҲ§ dy', xdxdy:'xВ·dx вҲ§ dy (eval at p)', neg:'вҲ’dx вҲ§ dy'}[pick.value];
  }

  function render(){
    svg.innerHTML='';
    // grid
    for(let i=-4;i<=4;i++){
      const [x1,y1]=toScr(i,-3), [x2,y2]=toScr(i,3);
      svg.appendChild(SVG('line',{x1,y1,x2,y2,stroke:'#1a2130','stroke-width':1}));
    }
    for(let j=-3;j<=3;j++){
      const [x1,y1]=toScr(-4,j), [x2,y2]=toScr(4,j);
      svg.appendChild(SVG('line',{x1,y1,x2,y2,stroke:'#1a2130','stroke-width':1}));
    }
    const [xL,yL]=toScr(-4,0), [xR,yR]=toScr(4,0);
    svg.appendChild(SVG('line',{x1:xL,y1:yL,x2:xR,y2:yR,stroke:'#2a3242','stroke-width':1.3}));
    const [xT,yT]=toScr(0,3), [xB,yB]=toScr(0,-3);
    svg.appendChild(SVG('line',{x1:xT,y1:yT,x2:xB,y2:yB,stroke:'#2a3242','stroke-width':1.3}));

    // parallelogram corners (math coords)
    const A = P, B = {x:P.x+V.x,y:P.y+V.y}, C = {x:P.x+V.x+Wv.x,y:P.y+V.y+Wv.y}, D = {x:P.x+Wv.x,y:P.y+Wv.y};
    const pts = [A,B,C,D].map(q=>toScr(q.x,q.y));
    const d = `M${pts[0][0]} ${pts[0][1]} L${pts[1][0]} ${pts[1][1]} L${pts[2][0]} ${pts[2][1]} L${pts[3][0]} ${pts[3][1]} Z`;
    const c = coef();
    const signed = c * (V.x*Wv.y - V.y*Wv.x);
    const col = signed >= 0 ? 'rgba(131,193,103,0.30)' : 'rgba(224,122,95,0.30)';
    const stk = signed >= 0 ? 'var(--green)' : '#e07a5f';
    svg.appendChild(SVG('path',{d, fill:col, stroke:stk, 'stroke-width':1.4, 'stroke-dasharray':'3,3'}));

    // basepoint
    const [bx,by]=toScr(P.x,P.y);
    svg.appendChild(SVG('circle',{cx:bx,cy:by,r:3.5,fill:'var(--ink)'}));

    drawArrow(svg,[bx,by],toScr(B.x,B.y),{color:'var(--blue)',width:2.2,markerId:'w2-av'});
    drawArrow(svg,[bx,by],toScr(D.x,D.y),{color:'var(--pink)',width:2.2,markerId:'w2-aw'});
    const [vx,vy]=toScr(B.x,B.y); const lv=SVG('text',{x:vx+8,y:vy+4,'font-size':13,fill:'var(--blue)','font-style':'italic'}); lv.textContent='v'; svg.appendChild(lv);
    const [wx,wy]=toScr(D.x,D.y); const lw=SVG('text',{x:wx+8,y:wy+4,'font-size':13,fill:'var(--pink)','font-style':'italic'}); lw.textContent='w'; svg.appendChild(lw);

    out.textContent =
      `Пү = ${label()}   (coefficient c at p = ${c.toFixed(3)})\n` +
      `v = (${V.x.toFixed(3)}, ${V.y.toFixed(3)}),  w = (${Wv.x.toFixed(3)}, ${Wv.y.toFixed(3)})\n` +
      `Пү(v,w) = c В· det[v | w] = ${c.toFixed(3)} В· (${(V.x*Wv.y - V.y*Wv.x).toFixed(3)}) = ${signed.toFixed(4)}\n` +
      `unsigned parallelogram area = ${Math.abs(V.x*Wv.y - V.y*Wv.x).toFixed(3)}`;
  }
  pick.addEventListener('change', render);
  render();
})();
</script>

4. The exterior derivative

The exterior derivative is a linear operator $d\colon \Omega^k(M)\to \Omega^{k+1}(M)$ satisfying three axioms that characterize it uniquely:

  • $df$ for $f\in\Omega^0$ is the ordinary differential (the $1$-form from В§2);
  • $d$ is a graded derivation: $d(\alpha\wedge\beta) = d\alpha\wedge\beta + (-1)^{|\alpha|}\alpha\wedge d\beta$;
  • $d^2 = 0$, i.e. $d(d\omega)=0$ for every form.

In coordinates on $\mathbb{R}^n$, write $\omega = \sum_I \omega_I\,dx^I$ for $I=(i_1<\cdots

$d\omega = \sum_I \sum_j \frac{\partial \omega_I}{\partial x^j}\,dx^j\wedge dx^I.$

Recovering grad, curl, div

On $\mathbb{R}^3$ with the usual metric, identify vector fields with $1$-forms via $X=(P,Q,R)\leftrightarrow \omega_X = P\,dx + Q\,dy + R\,dz$, and $2$-forms via the Hodge star: $\star\omega_X = P\,dy\wedge dz + Q\,dz\wedge dx + R\,dx\wedge dy$. Then the three classical operators are all incarnations of $d$:

classicalvia forms
$\nabla f$$df = f_x\,dx + f_y\,dy + f_z\,dz$
$\nabla \times X$$d\omega_X = (R_y-Q_z)\,dy\wedge dz + (P_z-R_x)\,dz\wedge dx + (Q_x-P_y)\,dx\wedge dy$
$\nabla\cdot X$$d(\star\omega_X) = (P_x + Q_y + R_z)\,dx\wedge dy\wedge dz$

Now $d^2=0$ reads: $\nabla\times(\nabla f)=0$ and $\nabla\cdot(\nabla\times X)=0$. Two identities, one proof.

"$d$ is the boundary of the form." Stokes will explain this: $\int_M d\omega = \int_{\partial M}\omega$, so integrating $d\omega$ over a region is the same as integrating $\omega$ over the boundary. From this perspective, $d$ plays for forms what $\partial$ plays for chains. The relation $d^2=0$ is dual to $\partial\partial=0$ ("the boundary of a boundary is empty").
Widget В· exterior derivative calculatoron $\mathbb{R}^2$
Grid of the plane (centered at origin, minus the puncture at $(0,0)$ for the angle form). Each cell is colored by the sign and magnitude of the coefficient of $d\omega$ there, normalized. Green = positive, pink = negative, dark = near zero.

Used in
Differential forms В· Stokes theorem and de Rham cohomology

5. Integration of forms over chains

A smooth $k$-simplex in $M$ is a smooth map $\sigma\colon\Delta^k\to M$ from the standard $k$-simplex, and a smooth $k$-chain is a finite formal $\mathbb{Z}$-linear combination $c=\sum n_i\,\sigma_i$ of such. The integral of a $k$-form over a chain is defined by pullback to the standard simplex plus linearity:

$\displaystyle \int_\sigma \omega \;:=\; \int_{\Delta^k} \sigma^*\omega,\qquad \int_{\sum n_i\sigma_i}\omega \;:=\; \sum n_i \int_{\sigma_i}\omega.$

Pullback-invariance is what makes this well-defined: reparametrizing $\sigma$ by an orientation-preserving diffeomorphism of $\Delta^k$ leaves the integral unchanged (Jacobian = $+1$ at the form level), while an orientation-reversing reparametrization flips the sign, matching the $\pm 1$ coefficient on $\sigma$ in the chain.

This is the bridge between В§4 and В§5 below: integrating a $(k{-}1)$-form over the boundary chain $\partial M$ and integrating $d\omega$ over the bulk $k$-chain $M$ are both defined via the same pullback mechanism вҖ” the only question is whether they agree. They do, and that statement is Stokes.

See also
  • Singular homology
Used in
Differential forms В· Stokes theorem and de Rham cohomology

5.1. Pullback of forms (aside)

For a smooth map $f\colon N\to M$, the pullback $f^*\colon\Omega^k(M)\to\Omega^k(N)$ acts on $k$-forms contravariantly. On a decomposable form,

$f^*(\omega_1\wedge\cdots\wedge\omega_k)(v_1,\ldots,v_k) \;=\; \omega_1\wedge\cdots\wedge\omega_k\bigl(df(v_1),\ldots,df(v_k)\bigr).$

Two identities make pullback the "right" operation:

  • $f^*(d\omega) = d(f^*\omega)$ вҖ” pullback commutes with the exterior derivative.
  • $f^*(\omega\wedge\eta) = (f^*\omega)\wedge(f^*\eta)$ вҖ” pullback is a ring map.

Together they say $f^*\colon \Omega^\bullet(M)\to\Omega^\bullet(N)$ is a map of differential graded algebras. This is why integration of forms is coordinate-invariant: changing coordinates is a pullback, and pulling back a top form along a diffeomorphism picks up the Jacobian determinant exactly once, giving the usual change-of-variables formula for free.

See also
  • Smooth maps and diffeomorphisms
Used in
Differential forms В· Integration of forms over chains

6. Stokes's theorem

Let $M$ be a compact oriented smooth $k$-manifold with boundary $\partial M$ (with induced orientation). For any $(k{-}1)$-form $\omega$ on an open neighborhood of $M$,

$$\int_{\partial M} \omega = \int_M d\omega.$$

This one identity specializes to several classical theorems you have already seen. Each row here is the same theorem, in different coordinates:

$(k, M)$namestatement
$k=1$, intervalFTC$\int_a^b f'(x)\,dx = f(b)-f(a)$
$k=2$, planar regionGreen$\oint_{\partial D}(P\,dx+Q\,dy) = \iint_D (Q_x - P_y)\,dA$
$k=2$, surface in $\mathbb{R}^3$Stokes (classical)$\oint_{\partial S} \vec F\cdot d\vec r = \iint_S (\nabla\times\vec F)\cdot d\vec S$
$k=3$, solid in $\mathbb{R}^3$Gauss$\iiint_V (\nabla\cdot\vec F)\,dV = \oiint_{\partial V}\vec F\cdot d\vec S$

The only thing to check in each case is that the coefficient of $d\omega$ is what the classical statement says вҖ” and that is the grad/curl/div dictionary of В§4.

What is a proof of Stokes? Cover $M$ by charts, use a partition of unity, reduce to the model case $M = [0,1]^k$ with $\omega$ supported in the interior (trivial) or with $\omega$ having exactly one boundary face contributing (one-dimensional FTC in the corresponding variable). The combinatorics of "which faces of the cube show up" is exactly antisymmetry.
Widget В· Stokes on a diskrefinement slider
40
$M$ is the unit disk. Left: boundary polygon with $N$ samples (midpoint rule for $\oint\omega$). Right: grid of $N\times N$ cells inside the disk, midpoint rule for $\iint d\omega$. Watch both values converge вҖ” and their difference shrink вҖ” as $N$ grows.

7. Closed vs exact вҖ” a first look at de Rham cohomology

The condition $d^2=0$ makes $(\Omega^\bullet(M), d)$ into a cochain complex:

$\Omega^0(M) \xrightarrow{d} \Omega^1(M) \xrightarrow{d} \Omega^2(M) \xrightarrow{d} \cdots.$

Call $\omega$ closed if $d\omega=0$, and exact if $\omega=d\eta$ for some $\eta$. Exact forms are closed (because $d^2=0$); the converse is the interesting question.

The de Rham cohomology of $M$ is

$H^k_{\mathrm{dR}}(M) \;=\; \dfrac{\ker\bigl(d\colon\Omega^k\to\Omega^{k+1}\bigr)}{\operatorname{im}\bigl(d\colon\Omega^{k-1}\to\Omega^k\bigr)}.$

A priori each $H^k$ is a real vector space defined with analytic objects. The deep fact (de Rham's theorem) is that $H^k_{\mathrm{dR}}(M) \cong H^k(M;\mathbb{R})$ вҖ” the same number you get from simplicial/singular cohomology. Closed-modulo-exact of smooth forms is a topological invariant.

PoincarГ© lemma

On any star-shaped open set $U\subseteq\mathbb{R}^n$ (i.e. there exists $x_0\in U$ with the segment from $x_0$ to any $x\in U$ contained in $U$), every closed form is exact: $H^k_{\mathrm{dR}}(U)=0$ for $k\ge 1$. The proof is a homotopy operator: integrating along the rays from $x_0$ produces a primitive explicitly.

In particular, $H^k_{\mathrm{dR}}(\mathbb{R}^n)=0$ for $k\ge 1$. All cohomology comes from topology, not from local analysis.

The annulus: the angle form $d\theta$

Let $M = \mathbb{R}^2\setminus\{0\}$ (or, equivalently for cohomology, any annulus). Consider

$\omega \;=\; \dfrac{-y\,dx + x\,dy}{x^2+y^2}.$

A direct computation (you saw it in В§4) shows $d\omega = 0$. In polar coordinates $\omega = d\theta$ locally, but $\theta$ is only defined modulo $2\pi$: there is no global function whose differential is $\omega$. Concretely, integrating along a loop winding once around the origin gives

$\oint \omega = 2\pi \neq 0,$

whereas an exact form would integrate to $0$ over any closed loop (by Stokes: $\oint d\eta = \int_{\partial(\text{loop})} d\eta$ вҖ” but a closed loop has empty boundary). So $\omega$ is closed but not exact. In fact $[\omega]$ generates $H^1_{\mathrm{dR}}(\mathbb{R}^2\setminus\{0\}) \cong \mathbb{R}$, and the cohomology class detects the winding number of loops around the origin.

Slogan. $H^k_{\mathrm{dR}}$ counts, in dimension $k$, the ways your manifold fails to be contractible. "Closed-not-exact" = "there is a hole."
Widget В· closed but not exactwinding number slider
1 0.90
Background: the angle form $d\theta = (-y\,dx + x\,dy)/(x^2+y^2)$ drawn as a vector field (blue). The inner and outer white circles bound an annulus; the yellow loop winds $n$ times around the puncture. Watch $\oint d\theta = 2\pi n$.

A compact cohomology table

space$H^0_{\mathrm{dR}}$$H^1_{\mathrm{dR}}$$H^2_{\mathrm{dR}}$
$\mathbb{R}^n$$\mathbb{R}$$0$$0$
$S^1$$\mathbb{R}$$\mathbb{R}$$0$
$S^2$$\mathbb{R}$$0$$\mathbb{R}$
$T^2 = S^1\times S^1$$\mathbb{R}$$\mathbb{R}^2$$\mathbb{R}$
$\mathbb{R}^2\setminus\{0\}$$\mathbb{R}$$\mathbb{R}$$0$
$\mathbb{R}^3\setminus\{0\}$$\mathbb{R}$$0$$\mathbb{R}$

Each $H^0 = \mathbb{R}^{\#\text{components}}$; that is just "locally constant functions." The first interesting data is in $H^1$, which counts $1$-dimensional holes.

Used in
Гүtale cohomology В· Comparison theorems: Г©tale vs. singular, de Rham, crystalline

7. Where this connects

Differential forms and $d$ are the common language of half of modern geometry. A few directions to follow:

Riemann surfaces and Hodge theory

On a compact oriented Riemann surface $X$ of genus $g$, the first de Rham cohomology $H^1_{\mathrm{dR}}(X)$ is $2g$-dimensional, and the Hodge decomposition $H^1 = H^{1,0}\oplus H^{0,1}$ splits it into holomorphic and antiholomorphic $1$-forms. The space of holomorphic $1$-forms is $g$-dimensional вҖ” this is the dimension of the genus, the number of "independent" closed-but-not-exact holomorphic forms. Most of classical complex analysis on surfaces is a special case of this picture.

Lie groups and MaurerвҖ“Cartan

On a Lie group $G$, the left-invariant $1$-forms form a copy of $\mathfrak{g}^*$. Putting them all together gives the MaurerвҖ“Cartan form $\theta\in\Omega^1(G;\mathfrak{g})$, and it satisfies

$d\theta + \tfrac{1}{2}[\theta,\theta] = 0,$

a first-order equation encoding the entire Lie algebra structure. Connections on principal bundles are "twisted" MaurerвҖ“Cartan forms; the failure of $d\theta + \tfrac{1}{2}[\theta,\theta]$ to vanish is the curvature.

Sheaf cohomology and ДҢech-de Rham

The de Rham complex is a resolution of the constant sheaf $\underline{\mathbb{R}}$ by fine sheaves, so $H^k_{\mathrm{dR}}(M) \cong H^k(M;\underline{\mathbb{R}})$ as computed by ДҢech cohomology on a good cover. This is how de Rham cohomology talks to algebraic topology. Shift from $\mathbb{R}$ to the sheaf of holomorphic functions and you get Dolbeault cohomology; shift to an algebraic setting and you get algebraic de Rham cohomology, then crystalline cohomology, and so on.

Physics

Gauge fields are connection $1$-forms; field strengths are curvature $2$-forms; Bianchi identities are $d^2=0$. Charge conservation is $d\star J = 0$. Topological charge (instanton number, Chern number) is the integral of an $n$-form over a closed manifold вҖ” a de Rham integral whose value is a topological invariant.

Recurring motif. Every time you meet a differential operator squaring to zero ($d$, $\partial$, $\bar\partial$, $\delta$, a BRST operator), you get a cohomology theory, and its classes see the global shape of whatever you are working on. Closed-modulo-exact is one of the most general and most useful ideas in mathematics.
